MOTION FOR LEAVE TO Amend Complaint; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Mar-13-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. DYLAN HACKETT, Esq. of The Hackett Law Firm (tel: 415-410-9931) for Plaintiffs.
Atty. LEON JEW, Esq. of Dayhee Law Group (tel: 925-463-3288) for Defendants.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFFS XIZHEN ZHU, AND YU LIN CHEN's MOTION FOR LEAVE TO Amend Complaint.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion for leave to amend the complaint is denied. Plaintiff has not complied with the requirements of 3.1324. In particular, Plaintiff's motion is not accompanied by a declaration from counsel and does not state when the facts giving rise to the amended allegations were discovered or why the amendment could not have been earlier made. The motion also does not specify what allegations are proposed to be added or deleted by page and line number.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling, with a cover letter reflecting compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Motion To Compel Plaintiff Xizhen Zhu To Arbitrate This Case, And Stay The Current Proceeding
Off Calendar Mar-05-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT KINGDOM OF DUMPLING LLC's Motion To Compel Plaintiff Xizhen Zhu To Arbitrate This Case, And Stay The Current Proceeding.

Defendant's motion to compel arbitration and stay the current proceedings is denied. Plaintiff contests the existence of an agreement to arbitrate. Even assuming there is an agreement to arbitrate, the court finds that the defendant has waived its right to compel arbitration.

The benefit of an arbitration agreement may be waived by a party. "In determining waiver, a court can consider (1) whether the party's actions are inconsistent with the right to arbitrate; (2) whether the litigation machinery has been substantially invoked and the parties were well into preparation of a lawsuit before the party notified the opposing party of an intent to arbitrate; (3) whether a party either requested arbitration enforcement close to the trial date or delayed for a long period before seeking a stay; (4) whether a defendant seeking arbitration filed a counterclaim without asking for a stay of the proceedings; (5) whether important intervening steps [e.g., taking advantage of judicial discovery procedures not available in arbitration] had taken place; and (6) whether the delay affected, misled, or prejudiced the opposing party." (St. Agnes Medical Center v. PacifiCare of California (2003) 31 Cal.4th 1187, 1196 [internal quotation marks and citation omitted; brackets in original].)

Here, Defendant answered the complaint on June 22, 2023 and did not assert that the court lacked jurisdiction because the parties had agreed to arbitrate. Defendant participated in litigation for over a year and invoked the authority of the court to obtain discovery from the Plaintiff in a recent motion to compel the Plaintiff's deposition. Trial is now set for March 17, 2025. At least the first three and possibly the fifth of the factors identified in St. Agnes are present. On these facts, the court finds that Defendant waived the benefit of the arbitration agreement, if it exists.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in copliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff Xizhen Zhu To Attend A Deposition, And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support
Off Calendar Feb-27-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Dylan Hackett Esq. THE HACKETT LAW FIRM (415) 410-9931 dylanhackett@hackettfirm.com Attorney for Plaintiffs
LEON E. JEW, Esq., DAYHEE LAW GROUP (925) 463-3288 Attorney for Defendants KINGDOM OF DUMPLING LLC, QINGHE LI and HUILI LI.(VIA ZOOM)

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT KINGDOM OF DUMPLING LLC's Motion To Compel Plaintiff Xizhen Zhu To Attend A Deposition.

Defendant Kingdom of Dumpling LLC moves to compel Plaintiff Xizhen Zhu to attend a deposition and for sanctions. The unopposed motion is GRANTED.

The Jew declaration establishes that Kingdom noticed Zhu's deposition for January 31, 2025. Zhu's counsel stated she would not be produced for deposition. The court finds Jew's meet and confer efforts reasonable. The court finds good cause for defendant Kingdom to depose plaintiff Zhu in this case and orders Zhu to attend a deposition, scheduled on a date convenient to the parties, within 10 court days of notice of entry of this order.

Kingdom further seeks sanctions. CCP 2025.450(g) provides that if a motion to compel a deponent to attend a deposition is granted, "the court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . in favor of the party who noticed the deposition and against the deponent or the party with whom the deponent is affiliated, unless the court finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." The court does not find substantial justification here. The court orders Zhu to pay $2250 to Kingdom within 10 court days of notice of entry of this order.

The prevailing party is to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)
